title: The Gang Buys a Roller Rink

jokes.text: Why would you deliver such sad news in such an upbeat fashion? / Uh, yeah, you know what? It felt wrong as I was doing it. Wasn't sure how to stop.
jokes.setup: Character delivers sad news about the rink closing while roller skating and being upbeat
jokes.punchline: Admits he knew it felt wrong but couldn't figure out how to stop the inappropriate tone
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor about awkward social situations, economically delivered with character-specific obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Why would you deliver such sad news in such an upbeat fashion?

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Uh, yeah, you know what? It felt wrong as I was doing it. Wasn't sure how to stop.

jokes.text: Everybody wants to pave paradise and put up a parking lot. / Nice! Nice. Uh, Joni Mitchell, yeah. I found a way to weave it in. I heard you do that.
jokes.setup: Charlie quotes Joni Mitchell lyrics in context of sad news
jokes.punchline: Mac congratulates him for awkwardly forcing in the reference from their earlier conversation
jokes.explanation: Clever callback that highlights the characters' social awkwardness and tendency to overthink references

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Everybody wants to pave paradise and put up a parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Nice! Nice. Uh, Joni Mitchell, yeah. I found a way to weave it in. I heard you do that.

jokes.text: Hotdogging happens at the concession stand.
jokes.setup: Rink manager warns against 'hotdogging' (showing off)
jokes.punchline: Delivers literal interpretation that hotdogging belongs at the food stand
jokes.explanation: Solid dad-joke style pun delivered with authority by period-appropriate character

dialogue.line: Hotdogging happens at the concession stand.

jokes.text: This jacket's phat as hell. / It may be phat, but you're not supposed to wear it, all right?
jokes.setup: Charlie defends his jacket using 90s slang 'phat'
jokes.punchline: Mac responds treating 'phat' as 'fat' while missing the slang entirely
jokes.explanation: Period-appropriate language confusion, though the pun is somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: This jacket's phat as hell.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: It may be phat, but you're not supposed to wear it, all right?

jokes.text: I got to look phat 'cause I'm out there selling weed, and you got to look phat if you're selling weed.
jokes.setup: Charlie explains fashion choices for work
jokes.punchline: Casually reveals he's dealing drugs at the family-friendly roller rink
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Charlie's warped priorities and casual criminality in an innocent setting

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I got to look phat 'cause I'm out there selling weed, and you got to look phat if you're selling weed.

jokes.text: And also, dude, I'm gonna say this as your friend and not just your boss... stop dealing drugs at work, okay?
jokes.setup: Mac tries to give Charlie friendly advice
jokes.punchline: The 'friendly' advice is just basic workplace conduct about not dealing drugs
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist workplace comedy - treating drug dealing as a minor HR issue

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: And also, dude, I'm gonna say this as your friend and not just your boss... stop dealing drugs at work, okay?

jokes.text: You know, Dennis looks like Jerry Seinfeld. / Thanks, man. Yeah, that's kind of what I was going for.
jokes.setup: Mac compares Dennis to Jerry Seinfeld
jokes.punchline: Dennis takes it as a compliment and claims it was intentional
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat showing Dennis's vanity and the period setting's pop culture references

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: You know, Dennis looks like Jerry Seinfeld.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Thanks, man. Yeah, that's kind of what I was going for.

jokes.text: I don't like how mean they are to each other on that show. / Aw, Sweet Dee. So pure of heart. You know?
jokes.setup: Dee innocently criticizes Seinfeld for being mean
jokes.punchline: Dennis sarcastically calls her 'pure of heart' when audience knows she'll become terrible
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony - young Dee seems sweet but viewers know her future awfulness

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I don't like how mean they are to each other on that show.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Aw, Sweet Dee. So pure of heart. You know?

jokes.text: Oh, no, Charlie, uh, size 11 in men's. / That's okay. Oh, right. You wear men's. Yeah. I totally forgot.
jokes.setup: Dee clarifies she needs men's size 11 skates
jokes.punchline: Charlie awkwardly acknowledges this like it's a known character trait
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor about Dee's masculine features, sets up later big feet joke

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Oh, no, Charlie, uh, size 11 in men's.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: That's okay. Oh, right. You wear men's. Yeah. I totally forgot.

jokes.text: Guys, thank you so much for not making fun of the size of her feet. They're—they're very big.
jokes.setup: Dennis thanks the guys for being nice about Dee's feet
jokes.punchline: Immediately ruins his own point by emphasizing how big her feet are
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis character moment - fake kindness immediately undermined by his own cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Guys, thank you so much for not making fun of the size of her feet. They're—they're very big.

jokes.text: Mark my words, nobody will ever use email. The Internet is a fad for nerds.
jokes.setup: Smokey mentions email addresses
jokes.punchline: Mac confidently predicts the Internet will never catch on
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony - audience knows how wrong this prediction is, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Mark my words, nobody will ever use email. The Internet is a fad for nerds.

jokes.text: Like, what kind of business is he in? / It's complicated, you know? There's—there's numbers and money, you know?
jokes.setup: Mac asks about Dennis's dad's business
jokes.punchline: Dennis gives hilariously vague answer that describes literally any business
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Dennis's cluelessness about his father's obviously shady business

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Like, what kind of business is he in?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: It's complicated, you know? There's—there's numbers and money, you know?

jokes.text: I definitely see myself running this place one day. / I'll be running a multi-million-dollar business, and you'll be working at a roller rink.
jokes.setup: Mac expresses ambition to run the roller rink
jokes.punchline: Dennis condescendingly contrasts his future success with Mac's 'lowly' rink job
jokes.explanation: Great dramatic irony - Dennis's smugness about the future when audience knows they'll end up co-owning a bar

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I definitely see myself running this place one day.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I'll be running a multi-million-dollar business, and you'll be working at a roller rink.

jokes.text: Mobile phones. Fads for yuppies. Mark my words, Charlie, those things will never catch on.
jokes.setup: Dennis gets a call on his mobile phone
jokes.punchline: Mac again confidently predicts technology won't catch on, with his 'mark my words' catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to the Internet joke, establishing Mac's pattern of terrible tech predictions

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Mobile phones. Fads for yuppies. Mark my words, Charlie, those things will never catch on.

jokes.text: Also, you keep saying that, and I don't know if you're, like, fully expecting me to, like, mark these words down 'cause I don't have, like, a pen on me.
jokes.setup: Mac keeps saying 'mark my words'
jokes.punchline: Charlie takes the phrase literally and wonders if he should be writing them down
jokes.explanation: Excellent literal interpretation humor - Charlie's childlike logic applied to common idiom

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Also, you keep saying that, and I don't know if you're, like, fully expecting me to, like, mark these words down 'cause I don't have, like, a pen on me.

jokes.text: How am I supposed to learn if you don't show me how to do it? / You don't know how? / No. I don't know anything.
jokes.setup: Dennis wants to learn his father's business
jokes.punchline: Frank is shocked Dennis doesn't know 'how' when referring to sex with prostitutes
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the prostitute revelation, plays on audience's wrong assumption about the 'business'

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: How am I supposed to learn if you don't show me how to do it?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You don't know how?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: No. I don't know anything.

jokes.text: I thought this was something you'd learn in school.
jokes.setup: Dennis doesn't know how to have sex
jokes.punchline: Frank assumes this would be taught in school curriculum
jokes.explanation: Funny absurdist logic from Frank treating sex education as standard curriculum

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I thought this was something you'd learn in school.

jokes.text: I feel like I just watched you have sex with a prostitute. / Yeah. Why? / What do you mean why?
jokes.setup: Dennis processes what he just witnessed
jokes.punchline: Frank's casual 'why?' response to his son's obvious distress
jokes.explanation: Excellent comedic timing and character work - Frank's complete cluelessness about appropriate boundaries

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I feel like I just watched you have sex with a prostitute.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Yeah. Why?

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: What do you mean why?

jokes.text: It was your idea. / I didn't want to watch you have sex. / I didn't want you to watch me have sex, either.
jokes.setup: Dennis protests about watching
jokes.punchline: Frank admits he also didn't want Dennis watching, raising questions about why it happened
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the uncomfortable situation with mutual admission of inappropriateness

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It was your idea.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I didn't want to watch you have sex.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I didn't want you to watch me have sex, either.

jokes.text: I don't even know what it is I'm being fired from.
jokes.setup: Frank fires Dennis from his mysterious job
jokes.punchline: Dennis realizes he never actually knew what the job was
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to the running gag about Dennis's cluelessness about his father's business

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: I don't even know what it is I'm being fired from.

jokes.text: I just sold two ounces of Compton Endo. / Holy shit. Whoa, dude, there's got to be, like, $80 in there. / It's $4,000, Charlie.
jokes.setup: Mac shows off his drug money to Charlie
jokes.punchline: Charlie drastically underestimates the amount, showing how naive he is about drug values
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast showing Charlie's innocence versus Mac's criminal aspirations

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I just sold two ounces of Compton Endo.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Holy shit. Whoa, dude, there's got to be, like, $80 in there.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: It's $4,000, Charlie.

jokes.text: Oh, got to tighten them Dee's nuts. / Dee's nuts. / I'm sorry. I just don't find crass humor that funny.
jokes.setup: Charlie makes an accidental innuendo about axle nuts
jokes.punchline: Dee primly disapproves of the crude humor while Charlie realizes what he said
jokes.explanation: Basic innuendo humor elevated slightly by Dee's innocent disapproval and Charlie's awkwardness

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh, got to tighten them Dee's nuts. / Dee's nuts.

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I'm sorry. I just don't find crass humor that funny.

jokes.text: Cool? Charlie, in 20 years, everybody's gonna have a beeper. Mark my words.
jokes.setup: Charlie admires Mac's beeper
jokes.punchline: Mac predicts beepers will be universal, ironically just before cell phones make them obsolete
jokes.explanation: Another solid entry in Mac's hilariously wrong technology predictions series

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Cool? Charlie, in 20 years, everybody's gonna have a beeper. Mark my words.

jokes.text: Maybe I'll introduce myself, too? Put a name to the face 'cause customer service is important.
jokes.setup: Mac explains drug dealing procedure
jokes.punchline: Charlie wants to apply customer service principles to illegal drug transactions
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - Charlie's innocent work ethic applied to criminal activity creates perfect absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Maybe I'll introduce myself, too? Put a name to the face 'cause customer service is important.

jokes.text: They will return, no matter what, because they're addicted to the drugs. / Oh. That's how it works. / Oh. Okay, cool.
jokes.setup: Charlie worries about customer retention
jokes.punchline: Mac casually explains drug addiction as a business model, Charlie accepts this cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny business logic applied to addiction, with Charlie's innocent acceptance making it even more disturbing

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: They will return, no matter what, because they're addicted to the drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Oh. That's how it works. / Oh. Okay, cool.

jokes.text: Hi. I'm Charlie. I'll be taking care of you guys this evening.
jokes.setup: Charlie approaches drug buyers
jokes.punchline: Introduces himself like a restaurant server to criminals
jokes.explanation: Perfect execution of Charlie's misplaced hospitality industry training in criminal context

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Hi. I'm Charlie. I'll be taking care of you guys this evening.

jokes.text: I'm a little thrown off. I was expecting you guys to be so much older than me.
jokes.setup: Charlie continues his customer service approach
jokes.punchline: Makes inappropriate small talk about the drug buyers' ages
jokes.explanation: Good extension of Charlie's inappropriate friendliness in criminal situation

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: I'm a little thrown off. I was expecting you guys to be so much older than me.

jokes.text: Sorry. It's my first night. I'm in training, so... thank you for your patience.
jokes.setup: Charlie drops drug bags during the deal
jokes.punchline: Apologizes like a new employee to increasingly impatient criminals
jokes.explanation: Escalating the customer service absurdity as the situation becomes more dangerous

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: Sorry. It's my first night. I'm in training, so... thank you for your patience.

jokes.text: That's a gat. I got a gat. / You're carrying around a gun handle?
jokes.setup: Mac tries to intimidate drug buyers with his 'gun'
jokes.punchline: It's revealed to be just a gun handle he found in the trash
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of action movie tropes - the threatening weapon is completely useless junk

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: That's a gat. I got a gat.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You're carrying around a gun handle?

jokes.text: It's still cool, though.
jokes.setup: Mac's 'gun' is exposed as trash
jokes.punchline: He still thinks the useless gun handle is cool
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat showing Mac's inability to admit when something has gone wrong

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: It's still cool, though.

jokes.text: All right, well, look on the bright side, Mac. We just made a quick 25 cents.
jokes.setup: Their drug deal was a complete disaster
jokes.punchline: Charlie optimistically focuses on finding a quarter
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Charlie's ability to find positivity in complete failure

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: All right, well, look on the bright side, Mac. We just made a quick 25 cents.

jokes.text: And I'm starting to think that my dad's a bad man.
jokes.setup: Dennis reflects on his father's prostitute business
jokes.punchline: Massively understates that his pimp father might be 'a bad man'
jokes.explanation: Excellent understatement - calling a pimp 'a bad man' with such uncertainty is perfectly naive

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: And I'm starting to think that my dad's a bad man.

jokes.text: Mark my words, you're probably never gonna see that guy again for the rest of your life.
jokes.setup: Dennis wants to avoid his father
jokes.punchline: Mac confidently predicts Dennis will never see Frank again (Frank becomes a main character)
jokes.explanation: Another hilariously wrong Mac prediction - Frank becomes central to the show's future

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Mark my words, you're probably never gonna see that guy again for the rest of your life.

jokes.text: What if we went into business, like, for ourselves? / Like we do what Dennis's dad does at work, right, but we do it with each other.
jokes.setup: Charlie suggests going into business together
jokes.punchline: Mac accidentally suggests they become prostitutes for each other
jokes.explanation: Great accidental innuendo that horrifies Dennis and shows Mac's cluelessness

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: What if we went into business, like, for ourselves?

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Like we do what Dennis's dad does at work, right, but we do it with each other.

jokes.text: From the machines and my paycheck and just, like, saving the quarters and shit, I've saved up, like, $57,000.
jokes.setup: Charlie mentions he has some money saved up
jokes.punchline: Reveals an impossibly large amount saved from minimum wage and quarters
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist escalation - the mundane quarter collecting becomes an impossible fortune

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: From the machines and my paycheck and just, like, saving the quarters and shit, I've saved up, like, $57,000.

jokes.text: II-When she asked me to tighten her skates, I secretly loosened them, you know, 'cause real friends don't slow each other down, right? They help them soar.
jokes.setup: Charlie explains helping Dee with her skates
jokes.punchline: Reveals he sabotaged her skates with twisted logic about friendship
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark character moment - Charlie's good intentions lead to actively harmful sabotage

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: II-When she asked me to tighten her skates, I secretly loosened them, you know, 'cause real friends don't slow each other down, right? They help them soar.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm fine, you small-haired cunt.
jokes.setup: Dennis asks if Dee is okay after her fall
jokes.punchline: Sweet, innocent Dee suddenly becomes vicious and insulting
jokes.explanation: Shocking character transformation - the head injury reveals Dee's true awful nature in one perfect line

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm fine, you small-haired cunt.

jokes.text: A breath mint 'cause your mouth smells like you just ate a giant slice of shit pizza.
jokes.setup: Gang asks if Dee wants to be a partner
jokes.punchline: Dee delivers an elaborately disgusting insult
jokes.explanation: Continues Dee's transformation with gross-out humor showing her emerging cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: A breath mint 'cause your mouth smells like you just ate a giant slice of shit pizza.

jokes.text: I'm gonna get rich, I'm gonna get famous, and I'm probably gonna marry Scott Wolf.
jokes.setup: Dee explains her Hollywood dreams
jokes.punchline: Ends with specific dated reference to 90s heartthrob Scott Wolf
jokes.explanation: Good period-specific humor showing Dee's delusional aspirations with dated pop culture reference

dialogue.speaker: Dee
dialogue.line: I'm gonna get rich, I'm gonna get famous, and I'm probably gonna marry Scott Wolf.

jokes.text: Sweet Dee is not being very sweet.
jokes.setup: Dee has been incredibly vicious and cruel
jokes.punchline: Dennis mildly observes she's not living up to her 'Sweet' nickname
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective understatement and wordplay on her nickname after her vicious outburst

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Sweet Dee is not being very sweet.

jokes.text: I don't own this place. I just work here for the free parking.
jokes.setup: Gang assumes Smokey owns the roller rink he's trying to sell
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's just an employee who gets free parking
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection revealing their fundamental misunderstanding of the situation

dialogue.speaker: Smokey
dialogue.line: I don't own this place. I just work here for the free parking.

jokes.text: I own the bar around the corner. / There's a bar around the corner? / Yeah. That place is a real dump.
jokes.setup: Smokey mentions his bar
jokes.punchline: Immediately describes his own business as 'a real dump'
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor that foreshadows the terrible condition of what becomes Paddy's

dialogue.speaker: Smokey
dialogue.line: I own the bar around the corner.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: There's a bar around the corner?

dialogue.speaker: Smokey
dialogue.line: Yeah. That place is a real dump.

jokes.text: We'll take it. Any price. / Well, well, hang on a second. We don't know if we'll take it. We haven't even seen it yet. / Stop saying that. You are a terrible negotiator.
jokes.setup: Dennis immediately commits to buying the unknown bar
jokes.punchline: Mac calls out Dennis's terrible negotiating while having no negotiating skills himself
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing their incompetence at basic business decisions

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: We'll take it. Any price.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Well, well, hang on a second. We don't know if we'll take it. We haven't even seen it yet.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Stop saying that. You are a terrible negotiator.

jokes.text: Well, there's a palpable sadness in here.
jokes.setup: Gang surveys the terrible bar they just bought
jokes.punchline: Dennis uses pretentious language to describe the obviously depressing atmosphere
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dennis's tendency to over-intellectualize obvious situations

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Well, there's a palpable sadness in here.

jokes.text: Guys, mark my words, when we're finished with this place, you're not even gonna recognize it.
jokes.setup: Mac promises to transform the terrible bar
jokes.punchline: Final 'mark my words' prediction that will prove completely wrong
jokes.explanation: Perfect capstone to Mac's wrong predictions - the bar remains essentially unchanged

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: Guys, mark my words, when we're finished with this place, you're not even gonna recognize it.

jokes.text: I got a lot of things wrong. Yeah. We really didn't change that much. No. You haven't even changed clothes, for instance. You're still wearing the same sweatshirt.
jokes.setup: Present-day Mac admits his predictions were wrong
jokes.punchline: Charlie points out Mac is literally wearing the same clothes decades later
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor acknowledging the show's static nature - even the costumes haven't changed

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: I got a lot of things wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Yeah. We really didn't change that much.

dialogue.speaker: Mac
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Charlie
dialogue.line: You haven't even changed clothes, for instance. You're still wearing the same sweatshirt.
